does: This paper introduces CSRT, a red-teaming framework that synthesizes code-switching queries to probe safety vulnerabilities in large language models. Code-switching—mixing multiple languages within single prompts—is used systematically to elicit undesirable behaviors that standard monolingual attacks fail to trigger. The authors conduct comprehensive experiments across ten state-of-the-art LLMs using queries that combine up to ten languages, demonstrating that code-switching attacks achieve 46.7% higher success rates than English-only attacks while remaining effective across conventional safety domains. They also evaluate models' abilities to generate and understand code-switched text, and demonstrate that CSRT can generate attacks using only monolingual training data, validated through ablation studies examining the contribution of code-switching components.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research in LLM red-teaming and adversarial evaluation, extending prior monolingual safety testing methods to the multilingual setting. It responds to earlier multilingual red-teaming approaches that treated languages separately rather than exploiting the unique vulnerabilities created by language mixing. The paper connects to research on code-switching in natural language processing, which has established that mixing languages is common in multilingual communities but understudied in LLM safety. It also relates to broader work examining how LLMs handle linguistic diversity and potential gaps in safety alignment across languages, building on observations that safety guardrails are often weaker in non-English languages.
enables: This work opens several research directions in multilingual AI safety. It establishes code-switching as a systematic attack surface that developers must address when deploying LLMs in multilingual populations, suggesting future work on code-switching-aware safety training and alignment methods. The framework enables development of more robust multilingual safety benchmarks and could inspire defenses that explicitly account for language mixing. Beyond safety, the paper motivates investigation into why LLMs are vulnerable to code-switching attacks, potentially revealing fundamental issues with how models process and align across multiple languages. It also sets up opportunities to study whether similar mixing-based attacks work for other modalities or problem domains, and whether better multilingual pretraining and alignment techniques can reduce these vulnerabilities.
